sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Cerebrovascular Accident (Stroke)**
A stroke is a sudden neurological deficit caused by interruption of blood flow to the brain (ischemic stroke) or bleeding into the brain (haemorrhagic stroke). It is a medical emergency.

**Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)**
A TIA is a temporary episode of neurological dysfunction caused by a brief interruption of blood flow to the brain, without permanent brain injury. Symptoms resolve within 24 hours, often within minutes. TIAs are a significant risk factor for future stroke.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden onset of neurological symptoms, which may include:
  - Weakness, numbness or paralysis of the face (facial droop, asymmetry)
  - Arm or leg weakness or numbness (especially on one side of the body)
  - Speech difficulties (slurred speech, difficulty finding words, or inability to speak)
  - Vision changes (sudden loss of vision, double vision, or visual field deficit)
  - Balance or coordination problems (dizziness, vertigo, or ataxia)
  - Severe headache (especially sudden and severe, which may suggest haemorrhagic stroke)
  - Dysphagia, difficulty in swallowing
  - Altered level of consciousness (most common in haemorrhagic stroke due to increased ICP and brainstem involvement)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Other conditions that can mimic a stroke must be considered:
  - Hypoglycaemia
  - Intracerebral lesions (tumours, abscesses)
  - Seizures and post ictal states
  - Hemiplegic Migraines
  - Electrolyte Abnormalities (hyponatraemia)
  - Syncope
  - Sepsis
  - Subdural Haematoma
  - Vertigo, Labyrinthitis, Vestibular Neuritis
  - Confusion/cognitive dysfunction

- Functional Neurological Disorder Syndrome

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Oxygen if indicated
- Consider patient position (45 degrees, head up)
- Complete Vital Sign Survey, especially BGL
- Conduct (BE)FAST assessment If
- FAST- but history is suggestive of TIA, the patient is at risk and should still be considered for off-site transfer and hospital assessment at an appropriate facility
- Consider cranial nerve assessment
- Consider RACE score
- FAST+, stroke is the likely diagnosis; conduct the RACE assessment tool to identify Large Vessel Occlusion
- Clinicians should have a very low threshold for calling the on-call doctor and alert RFDS as this cohort of patients will require transfer to a suitable acute stroke centre
- Keep patient warm and comfortable

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Cardiac monitoring (arrythmias), 12 LEAD ECG if concurrent chest pain and/or time permits
- Consider advanced airway if required
- Gain IV Access, 2 x large bore
- Collect pre-hospital blood sample for i-STAT testing where appropriate
- Consider IV fluids
- Consider benzodiazepines if actively seizing, (titrated to effect), in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider prophylactic antiemetic to reduce aspiration risk
- Consider appropriate analgesia in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Maintain a high index of suspicion of CVA in persons presenting with neurological symptoms over the past 24 hours, excluding obvious causes for these neurological symptoms such as hypoglycaemia

- Patients should have continuous limb lead cardiac monitoring throughout to capture arrythmias, specifically AF which increases the risk of stroke, do not delay transfer / transport to capture a 12 lead unless there are specific reasons such as concurrent chest pain
- The timing of the onset of symptoms needs to be clearly defined so that the suitability for IV thrombolysis (≤9 hours) or Endovascular Treatment (within 24 hours) can be determined
